report.title: Anonymous-function `_` form in `endpoint!`/`outpoint!` was silently dead â€” `_` was matched as `Punct`, not `Ident`, so it fell through to literal use and emitted `fn _<HTTP>(...)` which Rust rejects
report.summary: All three macro-flavor parsers (`parse_trans`, `parse_semi_trans`, `parse_attr`) gated the auto-generated-name branch on `match_punct_consume(&mut tokens, "_")`. Rust's lexer produces `_` as a `TokenTree::Ident` (with `to_string() == "_"`), not a `TokenTree::Punct` â€” so the punct-match always returned `false`, the parser fell through to `expect_any_ident`, accepted `_` as the literal function name, and the codegen emitted `fn _<HTTP>(req: ...) { ... }` verbatim. `_` is a reserved identifier in Rust and cannot be the name of an item, so the user got `error: expected identifier, found reserved identifier \`_\`` at *their* macro call site â€” making the documented anonymous-function feature effectively unreachable.

impact: Anyone following the documented `_ <HTTP> { ... }` form (no explicit function name) â€” including the in-repo `example_hotaru/src/main.rs` after its recent rewrite â€” got a confusing rustc error pointing at the macro invocation with no hint that the framework's name-elision feature was the issue. The pattern was intended to let users register many one-off endpoints without inventing throwaway identifiers; instead every such site failed to compile. Even the workspace `cargo check --workspace` failed because `example_hotaru` itself exercises the feature.

behavior_diff.expected: Writing `_` in place of the function name causes the macro to substitute a unique generated ident (`auto_generated_<8-random-alpha>`), so registration runs against a private name the user does not need to think about. Documented intent: `endpoint!(APP.url("/"), middleware = [...], _ <HTTP> { ... })` should be valid.
behavior_diff.actual: At `hotaru_trans/src/url/parse.rs:29` (`parse_trans`), `:137` (`parse_semi_trans`), and `:217` (`parse_attr`), the dispatch was `match match_punct_consume(tokens, "_") { true => generated_name, false => expect_any_ident(...) }`. Because `_` is consumed by Rust's lexer as `TokenTree::Ident("_")`, the punct match never fired; control fell into `expect_any_ident`, which succeeded on the very same `_` token and produced the literal ident `_` as `fn_name`. Downstream `UrlFunc::generate_function` / `wrapper_function` / `__wrapper_<fn_name>` then emitted `fn _<HTTP>(req: ...)`, `__wrapper__`, `__register__` etc. â€” all of which rustc rejects with `expected identifier, found reserved identifier \`_\``.

status.fix: Swapped `match_punct_consume(..., "_")` for `match_ident_consume(..., "_")` at all three sites in `hotaru_trans/src/url/parse.rs` (lines 29, 137, 217). `match_ident_consume` already matches on `TokenTree::Ident(ident)` with `ident.to_string() == "_"`, which is exactly how the Rust lexer represents the underscore wildcard. The auto-generated-name branch now fires for `_` and the macro emits `fn auto_generated_<rand>(req: ...)` plus matching `__wrapper_auto_generated_<rand>` / `__register_auto_generated_<rand>`, all of which are valid Rust identifiers.
